caveats: a: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and covers the entire NAICS code, not the narrowed lens.
a: O*NET task data describe an occupation, not all occupations or firms in the industry.
a: Exposure is not realized labor reduction; implementation, quality control, and demand effects are excluded here.
rho: The cited AI samples are not an industry implementation study.
rho: The five-year period assumes continued model, security, and procurement progress but does not assume fully autonomous research delivery.

e: Occupation composition is an imperfect indicator of firm revenue model.

s5: Government-contract novation, key-person retention, and customer concentration can make a signed sale nontransferable in practice.

q: The result is especially sensitive to public-sector procurement rules and customer concentration.
d5: BLS projects employment, not revenue or constant-quality service quantity.
d5: The projection covers the full NAICS industry and a ten-year horizon, not the narrowed LMM lens and five-year horizon.

o: The share will vary sharply between regulated human-subject research, government evaluation, routine survey administration, and desk research.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Research and Development in the Social Sciences and Humanities

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat external research engagements, task orders, panels, program evaluations, surveys, and analytical deliverables, commonly priced as fixed-fee, time-and-materials, or reimbursable projects.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to repeat commissioned research contractors because the code's observed occupation mix includes counseling, clinical, education, fundraising, and health-service roles alongside research; these are not one coherent outsourced research-service model.
lens.excluded_activities: University and captive research units, grant-funded research without an external service customer, clinical or counseling service delivery, one-off self-directed research, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent firms conducting repeat commissioned social-science, behavioral, economic, policy, survey, evaluation, and humanities research for external government, nonprofit, and commercial customers.

a.bridge: S1 measures the 2023 employment and wages of all establishments in NAICS 541720, and S2 measures task content for social-science research assistants rather than the frozen contractor lens. I wage-weighted the observed occupation mix qualitatively: research assistance, data preparation, statistical analysis, reporting, software, office work, and project management are exposed, while field collection, client framing, subject interaction, methodological judgment, and accountable review are not fully substitutable. This can overstate exposure because S1 includes noneligible establishments and understate it because it omits self-employed owner work and newer AI-enabled analytical tasks.
caveats: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and covers the entire NAICS code, not the narrowed lens.
O*NET task data describe an occupation, not all occupations or firms in the industry.
Exposure is not realized labor reduction; implementation, quality control, and demand effects are excluded here.
a.change_evidence: A wage-billed task census from 15 to 20 LMM research contractors, segmented by data collection, analysis, reporting, quality review, and project management; client-approved AI workflow logs; and contract deliverable inventories.
a.rationale: S1 reports 31.50 percent of employment in life, physical, and social-science occupations, 15.17 percent in business and financial operations, 10.15 percent in computer and mathematical occupations, and 12.70 percent in office support. Within the industry, social-science research assistants alone represent 10.82 percent of employment; S2 documents exposed tasks including data cleaning, statistical analysis, reporting, databases, and clerical work. Material senior research judgment and fieldwork remain outside the opportunity.

d5.bridge: S5 projects NAICS 541720 wage-and-salary employment from 70.0 thousand in 2024 to 72.6 thousand in 2034, a 3.7 percent ten-year increase. I use the implied modest five-year direction as a proxy for constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current service basket in the narrowed lens, adjusting down for labor-saving technology and the difference between employment and service quantity. It can overstate demand if employment rises from lower productivity or understate it if automation lets more research be purchased with fewer workers.
caveats: BLS projects employment, not revenue or constant-quality service quantity.
The projection covers the full NAICS industry and a ten-year horizon, not the narrowed LMM lens and five-year horizon.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted bookings, backlog, recompete win rates, task-order volume, federal and state social-science R&D appropriations, and repeat-client spend from a representative contractor panel.
d5.rationale: The official projection implies only modest ten-year employment growth, so the central case is near-flat to slightly positive five-year service demand rather than rapid expansion. Research demand is supported by policy, evaluation, and commercial insight needs, while automation and clients' internal analytical capacity can constrain outsourced quantity.

caveats: No public source observed the eligibility definition at LMM-firm level.
Occupation composition is an imperfect indicator of firm revenue model.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level Census Business Register or D&B/PrivCo pull for NAICS 541720, revenue and employee size, ownership, government-contractor status, and service descriptions; followed by website and customer-contract review of the LMM population.
e.rationale: The LMM count is injected separately. Eligibility is below the full code because the narrowed lens excludes internal, university, grant-only, clinical/counseling, and nonrepeat-service activity; S1's heterogeneous occupation mix supports a substantial but uncertain exclusion. The estimate represents firms that plausibly have repeat external commissioned-research revenue and transferable operations.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541720 ($6,516,505,720) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541720 ($9,647,627,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $201,227 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541720); class <5: 1,062 firms, avg revenue/firm $375,004 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $58,688 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 244 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,139,684 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $178,361 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 184 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,368,761 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $370,711 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 196 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,014,362 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,097,748 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 62 firms, avg revenue/firm $35,454,306 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,548,599 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 39 firms, avg revenue/firm $127,242,462 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $19,913,445 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 258

caveats: This is an estimate of demand that still requires an operator, not a forecast of employment.
The share will vary sharply between regulated human-subject research, government evaluation, routine survey administration, and desk research.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and renewal-loss analysis distinguishing software self-service from outsourced work; product adoption data for survey, analytics, and research-agent tools; and contract scopes showing which work packages still require named professional accountability.
o.rationale: Most commissioned research still requires an accountable operator for problem framing, sampling, respondent or stakeholder interaction, methodological choices, privacy and ethics controls, interpretation, and delivery accountability. S2 shows that a core research-assistant role also contains data, analysis, reporting, and clerical tasks that software can absorb, limiting the operator-required share.

caveats: No observed industry-wide pass-through measure exists for the narrowed lens.
The result is especially sensitive to public-sector procurement rules and customer concentration.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level pricing and renewal data from comparable firms, including fixed-fee versus time-and-materials versus reimbursable mix, proposal win rates, labor-category rate cards, customer MFN clauses, and actual post-automation renewal margins.
q.rationale: Research contractors can initially retain workflow savings in fixed-fee and repeat task-order work, but project bidding, labor-hour billing, cost-reimbursable contracts, renewal repricing, and sophisticated procurement are likely to share a meaningful portion with customers. The range is a discounted five-year retention judgment after those effects, without embedding volume effects or implementation difficulty.

rho.bridge: S3 reports observed Claude task success and speedup across its own November 2025 user sample, while S4 reports organization-wide AI adoption and early agent use; neither measures five-year operational implementation in LMM social-science research contractors. I translate their evidence of strong performance on complex knowledge work but uneven, early autonomous deployment into the share of the a opportunity likely to survive workflow design, validation, privacy, procurement, and customer acceptance. This likely overstates implementation if contractor data are more sensitive than the source populations and understates it if tooling improves rapidly.
caveats: The cited AI samples are not an industry implementation study.
The five-year period assumes continued model, security, and procurement progress but does not assume fully autonomous research delivery.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after operating metrics from comparable contractors, including cycle time, labor hours per deliverable, error/rework rates, privacy incidents, customer acceptance, and AI governance approval rates; plus agency and university IRB or data-use requirements.
rho.rationale: S3 finds higher speedups on complex tasks and a 66 percent success rate for college-degree-level tasks, which supports assisted analysis and drafting. S4 reports broad organizational AI adoption but agent deployment still in single digits in nearly all functions. Research protocols, confidential respondent data, reproducibility, and client review constrain deployment beyond individual use.

caveats: No public dataset was found that measures qualifying control transfers for the frozen NAICS and EBITDA lens.
Government-contract novation, key-person retention, and customer concentration can make a signed sale nontransferable in practice.
s5.change_evidence: Brokered-deal and closed-transaction data from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, and SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, coded to 541720 and EBITDA band; owner-age and succession data from a targeted owner survey; and novation or consent records for government contracts.
s5.rationale: A qualifying control transfer requires a buyer, durable contracts, transferable personnel and methods, and acceptable customer or clearance transition terms. The base is a bounded five-year judgment for eligible LMM professional-service contractors, not a measured NAICS transfer rate; it excludes closures and internal reorganizations.

principal_driver: A large share of the operating work is digital research production, data handling, analysis, coordination, and reporting rather than physical delivery.
principal_weakness: The NAICS code is heterogeneous and no observed LMM data establish either transferable-firm supply or durable retention of AI-derived savings.
